topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up, bright and unwelcome, right in the middle of my study session. It was a direct message from Mr. Harrison. My blood instantly turned cold, then hot. I stared at the screen, my jaw clenching so hard I thought my teeth might crack. He had tagged me in a post about the class project, a casual acknowledgment, but the context of the message—a private, direct line—sent a tremor of pure indignation through me. I slammed my laptop shut, the sound echoing sharply in the quiet library corner. My hands were shaking, and I could feel the heat rising up my neck. I needed to find him, needed to confront this ridiculous invasion of my privacy, but all I could manage was a tight, ragged breath as I reread the screen one more time, the injustice burning behind my eyes.

[story 2]

I walked into the classroom feeling like I was walking into a trap. Every eye in the room seemed to be fixed on me, but it wasn't the students; it was the realization that Mr. Harrison had been watching. I felt a distinct tightening in my chest, a pressure building behind my sternum that made it hard to draw a full breath. When he finally spoke, his voice was annoyingly smooth, completely devoid of the warmth I’d always mistaken for mentorship. "Interesting perspective on the essay, Alex," he said, a small, knowing smile playing on his lips. The sheer audacity of it made my palms sweat. I gripped my pen so tightly the plastic creaked. I wanted to shout, to demand an explanation for this blatant overstep, but I kept my mouth shut, letting the silence stretch until it felt taut, vibrating with my barely contained anger.

[story 3]

It was late, and I was scrolling through my feed when I saw it: a picture of me from a school event, captioned with a comment only I would understand, posted by Mr. Harrison. A slow, simmering resentment began to build, starting low in my stomach and spreading outward until I felt physically unsteady. I stood up abruptly, knocking my chair slightly askew. The sound was loud, clumsy. I walked out of the room, my steps quick and uneven. Every glance from passing students felt like an accusation. I didn't look back. The image on my phone screen seemed to mock me, fueling a deep, internal churning. I walked toward the exit, my fists clenched at my sides, every muscle in my body coiled tight with a need to lash out at this unbelievable breach of trust.
